1: shadow As a deer pants for flowing streams, so pants my soul for you, O God. The spiritual thirst of exile finds its answer in Christ's invitation: If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink (John 7:37). Christ is the flowing stream — the one who satisfies the panting soul. He is also the deer himself, the righteous sufferer longing for the Father in his desolation.
2: shadow My soul thirsts for God, for the living God. When shall I come and appear before God? Christ on the cross cries I thirst (John 19:28), inhabiting the exile's longing at its depth. But he is simultaneously the living God (John 20:28 — my Lord and my God) for whom the soul thirsts. He thirsts as the human sufferer and satisfies as the divine source.
3: shadow My tears have been my food day and night, while they say to me all the day long, Where is your God? The taunt where is your God? is directed at Jesus on the cross: He trusts in God; let God deliver him now, if he desires him (Matthew 27:43). The tears of Gethsemane and the cross (Hebrews 5:7 — loud cries and tears) are the tears that become bread for all who mourn.
4: shadow These things I remember, as I pour out my soul: how I would go with the throng and lead them in procession to the house of God with glad shouts and songs of praise. Christ's triumphal entry into Jerusalem (Matthew 21:1-11) echoes this memory of leading the procession to the house of God with shouts of joy — Hosanna! — before the desolation of the Passion.
5: shadow Why are you cast down, O my soul, and why are you in turmoil within me? Hope in God; for I shall again praise him, my salvation. First refrain: Gethsemane's anguish — my soul is very sorrowful, even to death (Matthew 26:38). Christ commands his own soul to hope in God even in the depths of affliction. The resurrection is the praise that follows — I shall again praise him.
6: shadow My soul is cast down within me; therefore I remember you from the land of Jordan and of Hermon, from Mount Mizar. The geography of exile — Jordan, Hermon — becomes the geography of Christ's ministry and Transfiguration (Hermon is the likely site). He who remembered the Father in the wilderness (Matt 4) and on the mountain (Matt 17) takes up the exile's longing.
7: shadow Deep calls to deep at the roar of your waterfalls; all your breakers and your waves have gone over me. The cosmic depth of the atonement: the deep of human sin calls to the deep of divine suffering. Paul captures it: God made him to be sin who knew no sin (2 Corinthians 5:21) — the waters of divine judgment go over the one who descends into the deep for our sake.
8: revelation of God By day the LORD commands his steadfast love, and at night his song is with me, a prayer to the God of my life. God's chesed commanded by day and song by night reveals the God who is faithful through every darkness. Christ prays through the night (Luke 6:12) and declares the Father's steadfast love — he is the prayer to the God of life, the one who intercedes continuously (Hebrews 7:25).
9: shadow I say to God, my rock: Why have you forgotten me? Why do I go mourning because of the oppression of the enemy? The question echoes Psalms 22:1 — My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? — the cry Christ makes his own at Calvary (Matthew 27:46). God the rock appears to have forgotten the one who calls on him; the resurrection is the answer to this apparent abandonment.
10: shadow As with a deadly wound in my bones, my adversaries taunt me, while they say to me all the day long, Where is your God? The taunting during the Passion (Matthew 27:39-44): he trusts in God; let God deliver him. The deadly wound is literal in Christ's case — the wounds of the cross. The taunt becomes, through resurrection, the occasion for the ultimate answer: God raised him from the dead (Acts 2:24).
11: shadow Why are you cast down, O my soul, and why are you in turmoil within me? Hope in God; for I shall again praise him, my salvation and my God. The repeated refrain — Gethsemane's hope sustained through suffering. Christ's not my will, but yours, be done (Luke 22:42) is the faith-response embedded in this refrain: hope in God despite soul-anguish. The resurrection makes good the promise.
